questions.question: Why does tabular Q-learning die on real problems, and what's the "deadly triad"?
options: Tables are too fast to debug

A table needs one entry per (state, action) — chess has ~10⁴³ states, an Atari frame 100k features; but naively swapping in a neural net diverges under the deadly triad: function approximation + bootstrapping + off-policy learning
Q-learning can't handle rewards over 100
Tables don't fit in CPU cache
questions.explanation: Replacing the table with Q(s,a;θ) was obvious-in-hindsight but took decades — because the combination of the three triad ingredients is genuinely unstable without extra machinery.

questions.question: What are DQN's three stabilizing tricks?
options: Dropout, batch norm, weight decay

Experience replay (a ~10⁶ ring buffer sampled uniformly to decorrelate transitions), a target network (frozen copy θ⁻ refreshed every ~10k steps to stop the bootstrap target from moving), and reward clipping to {−1, 0, +1}
Bigger nets, more data, longer training
Curriculum, distillation, and ensembles
questions.explanation: With this three-trick base, one architecture and one hyperparameter set solved dozens of Atari games from raw pixels — the result that sparked the deep-RL era (Nature, 2015).

questions.question: Why exactly does the target network help?
options: It doubles model capacity

Using the same network on both sides of the Bellman equation means the regression target moves with every update — "chasing your own tail"; freezing θ⁻ for thousands of steps gives a stable target, with soft updates θ⁻ ← τθ + (1−τ)θ⁻ as the smoother variant
It parallelizes training across GPUs
It stores more transitions
questions.explanation: The bootstrap is the unstable triad ingredient you can directly tame: turn a moving-target regression into a sequence of fixed-target regressions.

questions.question: What does Double DQN fix, and how?
options: Slow inference, by pruning the net

Maximization bias: the max over noisy Q-estimates is biased upward — DDQN uses the ONLINE net to select the argmax action and the TARGET net to evaluate it; a drop-in change that is consistently better
Memory usage of the replay buffer
Exploration in sparse-reward games
questions.explanation: Hasselt (2016). Decoupling selection from evaluation breaks the upward bias loop — use it by default.

questions.question: What did Rainbow (2017) demonstrate about DQN improvements?
options: Most published improvements were fake
Prioritized replay, dueling V/advantage heads, noisy networks, n-step returns, and distributional Q each add a few percent — and the gains are roughly ADDITIVE when stacked
Only one trick matters at a time
DQN can't be improved further
questions.explanation: The lesson's framing: everything since — DDQN, Rainbow, R2D2, Agent57 — is engineering layered on the same three-trick base.
